content.text: Agentic tasks differ from single-shot LLM calls in one critical respect: they execute multiple LLM calls, tool calls, and retry loops before delivering an answer.
A task that works correctly but consumes 50,000 tokens and takes 40 seconds per run may be unusable in production.
Cost and latency are therefore first-class evaluation metrics, not afterthoughts.
The challenge is that cost and latency trade off against accuracy: a more expensive model with more steps usually gives better answers.
Evaluation must quantify that trade-off so you can make an informed choice — not just pick the cheapest model or the most accurate one in isolation.
Total token cost for one task run
Anthropic's API returns usage counts on every response object.
Wrap each agent run in a
across all calls, then converts to dollars using the model's published rates.
Running the same task suite with
Divide the accuracy score by cost to compute
— the metric that tells you when upgrading the model is economically justified.
Wall-clock latency is what the user experiences, but the breakdown matters for optimization.
If 80% of latency comes from tool calls, optimising the prompt won't help — you need faster tools or parallel execution.
If 80% comes from LLM generation, consider streaming or a smaller model.
Logging every span to an observability system (e.g. as structured JSON) lets you build percentile histograms across many task runs, which is more useful than single-run wall-clock times.
Marking the system prompt and tool schemas as cacheable means subsequent calls in the same session pay a lower
rate for those tokens rather than re-ingesting them as fresh input tokens.
For agents with long system prompts or many tool schemas, this can cut input token cost by 70–90%.
Cache reads cost approximately 10% of the standard input token rate.
When running evaluation suites with many tasks sharing the same system prompt, enabling caching can reduce the total evaluation cost by an order of magnitude.
Cost and Latency Budgets in Evaluation
Rather than measuring cost and latency as free-floating metrics, set explicit budgets and treat violations as test failures.
per task or takes more than 30 seconds has failed a budget constraint just as surely as it fails when it returns a wrong answer.
Mid-run budget checks abort tasks early when they exceed limits, preventing runaway costs during evaluation sweeps.
alongside task-success results to build a Pareto frontier: which model/prompt combinations achieve the best accuracy within the target budget?
Cost and latency are constraints, not nice-to-haves.
Measure them per-call and per-task, set explicit budgets, enforce them in your evaluation harness, and track them over time alongside accuracy —
the right optimisation target is accuracy per dollar at acceptable latency, not accuracy alone.
